metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: The run-validity guard (>20% INDETERMINATE triggers invalidation requiring a re-run) is described in ## What but no AC verifies that the harness actually ENFORCES the guard — i.e., that it refuses to write or marks invalid `e2_baseline.json` results when the cap is exceeded. AC4's verify script only checks that fields exist and vocabulary is correct, not that a validity-guard breach blocks `meets_threshold` from being set True.
findings.location: ## What (AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling, run-validity guard) vs AC4 (run_e2_benchmark.py)
findings.suggested_fix: In e2_baseline.json, add a top-level `run_valid: bool` field and a per-class `validity_guard_breached: bool`. Extend AC4's prove step to assert `b[k].get('validity_guard_breached', False) == False` for all classes in the committed baseline, and document that a breached run must not be committed as the final baseline.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or extend AC 4 to verify that `run_e2_benchmark.py` validates each target criterion family ID against `plan_review.registry.by_id` at startup and exits non-zero if any ID is absent from the live registry.
evidence: No AC checks that the harness imports or calls plan_review.registry.by_id
## Scope lists deliverables for run_e2_benchmark.py without mentioning registry validation
The described work (guard against non-existent criterion IDs) has no covering acceptance criterion
findings.finding: The ## What section describes runtime validation of criterion-family IDs against `plan_review.registry.by_id` ('each validated against the live registry at run time so no non-existent criterion id is asserted'), but no AC verifies this validation occurs, and the Scope does not name it as an explicit component of either harness.
findings.location: ## What (criterion-family attribution paragraph) — 'each validated against the live registry (plan_review.registry.by_id) at run time'
scenarios: A criterion ID in the detection table is silently renamed in the gate between E2 and a later R-story; the harness attributes detections to the old ID without error, corrupting the baseline table.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the `classify_pair` protocol: (1) the type of `target_criteria` (e.g. `set[str]` of criterion IDs); (2) which verdict buckets are searched (e.g. `blocking + advisory + overflow + indeterminate`); and (3) the explicit treatment of `overflow`-bucket target-family findings (detected or not).
evidence: Plan defines: 'classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)' with no type annotation or structural description of `target_criteria`
Live gate verdict dict confirmed to include keys: blocking[], advisory[], overflow[], indeterminate[], dropped[], coaching[] (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:531-544)

Plan's detection rule says 'a target-family finding surfaced with `decision ∈ {block, advisory}`' but 'overflow' findings are advisory-cap overflow — a legitimate target-family finding that exceeded the cap would be missed silently
Plan's blocking-FP rule says 'decision == block on the unmutated original' but does not say classify_pair ignores overflow/dropped findings on controls
findings.finding: `classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)` is defined with a `target_criteria` parameter whose type/form is never specified, and the plan never states which verdict bucket(s) the function inspects to find target-family findings. The live gate verdict has six finding buckets (`blocking`, `advisory`, `overflow`, `indeterminate`, `dropped`, `coaching`), but the plan only mentions `decision ∈ {block, advisory}` on findings without specifying whether `overflow` and `dropped` findings are also inspected.
findings.location: ## What — classify_pair definition: 'A pure helper `classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)` applies'; detection/blocking-FP rules
scenarios: A class-2 layer-cake mutation triggers a G3/G4 finding but the advisory cap is hit and it lands in `overflow[]` — classify_pair counts it as FN (not detected) without any acknowledgment that this is the intended behavior
`target_criteria` is passed as a set of strings by the harness author but as a list by the test author — classify_pair has no contract to validate against
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ## What: '`target_criteria: set[str]` — the plan searches `blocking + advisory + overflow` for target-family findings (a finding in `overflow` DOES count as detected, because advisory-cap overflow is an advisory finding the gate surfaced; only `dropped` and `coaching` are excluded). `indeterminate`-decision findings are per the INDETERMINATE rule above.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `run_validity` INDETERMINATE fraction definition: specify whether the numerator counts (a) rows where the top-level `verdict == 'INDETERMINATE'`, or (b) rows where the verdict OR any target-family finding has `decision == 'indeterminate'`, and align the `test_e2_counting.py` fixture to exercise both cases.
evidence: Plan defines: 'the INDETERMINATE fraction over mutants+controls and `valid = frac <= cap`' — no definition of what 'INDETERMINATE fraction' counts

Live gate: a PASS verdict can contain findings with `decision == indeterminate` in the `indeterminate[]` bucket (confirmed: orchestrator.py:448, sidecar.py:332 'decision in {"block", "advisory"} … excludes "dropped"/"indeterminate"')
A BLOCK verdict with 2 indeterminate findings is NOT a top-level INDETERMINATE verdict — counting it as INDETERMINATE vs not INDETERMINATE changes the fraction
The `classify_pair` INDETERMINATE rule says 'INDETERMINATE verdict … counts as NOT DETECTED' (top-level verdict) but `run_validity` says 'INDETERMINATE fraction' without specifying the same scope
findings.finding: The `run_validity` INDETERMINATE-fraction numerator is ambiguous: the plan says 'the INDETERMINATE fraction over mutants+controls' but the live gate emits both top-level `verdict == INDETERMINATE` AND per-finding `decision == indeterminate` within a PASS/BLOCK verdict. These are different counts, and the plan does not specify which is used as the numerator for the cap check.
findings.location: ## What — run_validity definition: '`run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=0.20)` computes, per class, the INDETERMINATE fraction over mutants+controls'
scenarios: A class-3 run produces 18 PASS/BLOCK verdicts each with 1 indeterminate finding + 2 top-level INDETERMINATE verdicts: top-level fraction = 2/20 = 10% (valid), per-finding fraction could be much higher (invalid) — the two implementations disagree on validity
The golden test for `run_validity` in `test_e2_counting.py` is written against whichever interpretation the implementer chose, silently encoding the wrong semantics
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the run_validity definition: 'INDETERMINATE fraction = (count of rows where top-level `verdict == INDETERMINATE`) / (total mutant + control rows in the class). Per-finding indeterminate decisions within a PASS/BLOCK verdict do NOT count toward this fraction — only top-level verdict INDETERMINATE does, consistent with the classify_pair INDETERMINATE rule.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify which existing criterion (P6 or an LLM-tier criterion) actually fires on an AC that requires out-of-codebase evidence but lacks `[operator-attested]`, and update the class-5 criterion-family row and the detection-tier column to match the code (or add a note that class-5 detection relies on the LLM tiers, not P6).
evidence: p6_ac_quality (det_floor.py:686-745) checks compound-AND, vague_lexicon, and verify-command presence — no operator-attested detection
_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE and enrich_operator_attested in workflow_ops.py:54-119 are false-positive suppressors, not detectors of missing tags
Plan states: 'untagged operational AC — an AC whose completion evidence lives outside the codebase, with the [operator-attested] tag stripped' — the stripped-tag condition is not checked by P6
CANONICAL_DET in registry.py:78 lists P1-P9; none has operator-attested detection logic
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'P6' (the DET-floor AC-quality check) as the detection criterion family for class-5 (untagged operational AC), but p6_ac_quality only checks for compound-AND items, vague lexicon, and presence of verify commands — it has no logic that specifically detects an AC whose completion evidence lives outside the codebase but lacks the [operator-attested] tag. The [operator-attested] infrastructure in workflow_ops.py is a false-positive suppressor (clears ac_unverifiable when the tag IS present), not a detector of missing tags. P6 cannot reliably attribute a flag to the class-5 defect by design.
findings.location: ## What — criterion family table, class 5 row ('| 5 untagged operational AC | `P6` | DET floor |')
scenarios: A class-5 mutant (tag stripped) is run through the gate; P6 fires only on vague lexicon or missing verify commands that coincidentally exist — not on the stripped tag itself. The discrimination number is therefore attributable to P6 incidentally, not by design, inflating apparent precision.
If the base plan's operational AC happens to have a concrete verify command already, P6 will not flag it at all, producing a FN that the plan does not account for.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '`P6` | DET floor' in the class-5 row with the LLM criterion that handles in-session-unverifiable ACs (likely a T-overlay or the BROAD pass), and verify against the live registry that such a criterion has an `ac_unverifiable` axis probe. If no criterion currently detects stripped tags, document the expected low detection rate explicitly.

findings.finding: The run-validity guard ('a run is INVALID and must be re-run if >20% of a class's mutants OR controls return INDETERMINATE') is a decision rule specified in ## What, but no output state is defined for a breached run — no `validity_guard_breached` field in `e2_baseline.json`, no exit-code behavior, and AC4's prove step only checks field presence, not guard enforcement.
findings.location: ## What — 'Run-validity guard' sub-section; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (`run_e2_benchmark.py` prove step); ## Scope — `e2_baseline.json` schema
findings.suggested_fix: In `e2_baseline.json`, add `{run_valid: true, classes: {"1": {…, validity_guard_breached: false}, …}}`. Extend the AC4 one-liner: `assert b_top.get('run_valid') is True` and `assert all(not b[k].get('validity_guard_breached') for k in b)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC1 proving command to also assert that every control `pair_id` has exactly one matching mutant `pair_id` in the same defect class, e.g. add: `pairs_m=set(r['pair_id'] for r in rows if r['role']=='mutant'); pairs_c=set(r['pair_id'] for r in rows if r['role']=='control'); assert pairs_m==pairs_c, (pairs_m-pairs_c, pairs_c-pairs_m)`.
evidence: AC text: 'each paired with exactly one CONTROL row, every row carrying `pair_id`'
Proving command: `assert all(m[k]>=10 and m[k]==ctl[k] for k in m)` — checks only that counts match, not that `pair_id` values are shared between mutant and control rows
Verify-command defect taxonomy type 4: fixture invalidity — the command does not exercise the pairing claim
findings.finding: AC1's proving command verifies that mutant and control COUNTS are equal per class but does not verify the pairing relationship — that each control's `pair_id` matches exactly one mutant's `pair_id`. An executor could create 10 mutants and 10 controls with entirely different `pair_id` values (no actual pairs) and the assertion would pass, violating the 'each mutant paired with exactly one control' requirement that the discrimination analysis depends on.
findings.location: AC1 — proving command (first checklist item)
scenarios: Corpus assembler generates controls from a different base ticket pool than the mutants; counts match but pairs don't; downstream classify_pair comparisons are meaningless because mutant and control came from different plans
findings.suggested_fix: Append the following to the AC1 one-liner before the final `print`: `; pairs_m={r['pair_id'] for r in rows if r['role']=='mutant'}; pairs_c={r['pair_id'] for r in rows if r['role']=='control'}; assert pairs_m==pairs_c, ('unpaired pair_ids', pairs_m^pairs_c)`

findings.finding: The degradation ladder describes reporting synthetic-base mutants separately (`synthetic_base: true`, "report those numbers separately from the real-base classes") but neither AC1 (corpus schema check) nor any other AC verifies that (a) the field exists on synthetic rows or (b) the baseline table distinguishes real-base vs synthetic-base detection rates if the ladder is invoked.
findings.location: ## What (Degradation if a class cannot reach 10 real base plans) vs ACs

findings.finding: AC1's proving command counts rows by defect_class and role but does NOT verify that every row contains all required fields (`pair_id`, `defect_class`, `role`, `source_ticket_id`, `plan_text`, `children`, and for mutants `mutation_op` + `mutation_diff`). A corpus with rows missing `mutation_diff` or `children` would pass the proving command yet violate the AC's stated requirement.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — first AC (corpus shape check), proving command
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the one-liner with a version that also checks: `required_m={'pair_id','defect_class','role','source_ticket_id','plan_text','children','mutation_op','mutation_diff'}; required_c=required_m-{'mutation_op','mutation_diff'}; assert all(required_m<=set(r) for r in rows if r['role']=='mutant') and all(required_c<=set(r) for r in rows if r['role']=='control'), 'field schema violation'`

findings.finding: AC1's verify script checks mutant counts per class but does NOT verify that mutant rows actually carry the mutant-specific fields `mutation_op` and `mutation_diff`. The script only checks field presence for ALL rows via the outer key list, and the assertion `m[k]>=10 and m[k]==ctl[k]` only counts rows — a mutant row missing `mutation_op`/`mutation_diff` would pass AC1 silently.
findings.location: AC1 prove step (the python3 -c one-liner)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the existing one-liner: `assert all('mutation_op' in r and 'mutation_diff' in r for r in rows if r['role']=='mutant'), 'mutant missing mutation_op/diff'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `--self-check-isolation` paragraph to the `run_e2_benchmark.py` scope entry specifying that when this flag is passed the script runs the gate on a single mutant/control pair and exits, enabling the HEAD-comparison isolation proof in AC6.
evidence: Scope for `run_e2_benchmark.py`: 'Clones REAL_REPO/.tickets-tracker → a tmp mutant store, injects each seeded defect… then per mutant/control runs the CURRENT gate… journals raw verdicts… computes run_validity… emits e2_baseline.json…' — no mention of --self-check-isolation
AC6: '.venv/bin/python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation' — the proving command calls a flag the scope never specifies
E6 checklist criterion (c): every acceptance criterion maps to at least one described step
findings.finding: The `--self-check-isolation` flag required by AC6's proving command is mentioned only in the AC itself; the ## Scope description of `run_e2_benchmark.py` never mentions this flag or its behavior (single-pair run that leaves real-store HEAD unchanged). An executor implementing the script from the scope description would not know to implement this flag, causing AC6's proving command to fail at runtime.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` description; AC6 proving command
scenarios: Executor implements `run_e2_benchmark.py` faithfully per the scope, omits `--self-check-isolation`, AC6 command exits with 'unrecognized argument' or silently skips the isolation check

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--verify-clean` to the `assemble_e2_mutants.py` description in ## Scope, specifying that it cross-checks each selected base ticket's `outcome_corpus.jsonl` row against the 5-condition filter and exits non-zero naming the first violating ticket.
evidence: Scope: '--dry-run prints the selected base pool without writing' — no mention of --verify-clean

AC 2: '.../assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean && echo FILTER-OK' with '(exits 0 only if every selected base ticket's corpus row passes the 5-condition filter; non-zero names the first violating ticket)'
The flag's semantics appear only in the AC parenthetical.
findings.finding: The `--verify-clean` flag is referenced in AC 2's proof command (`assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean`) but is nowhere described in the ## Scope section's description of `assemble_e2_mutants.py`, leaving its behavior under-specified for the implementer.
findings.location: ## Scope (assemble_e2_mutants.py description) vs. AC 2 proof command
scenarios: Implementer builds --dry-run correctly but omits --verify-clean, causing AC 2 to fail at evaluation time with a flag-not-found error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--self-check-isolation` to the `run_e2_benchmark.py` description in ## Scope, specifying that it runs a single mutant/control pair against a known fixture ticket and asserts the real `.tickets-tracker` HEAD is unchanged before and after.
evidence: Scope describes run_e2_benchmark.py's behavior (clone, inject, run gate, classify, journal) with no mention of --self-check-isolation
AC 6: '.venv/bin/python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation' — used to verify store isolation
findings.finding: The `--self-check-isolation` flag is referenced in AC 6's proof command (`run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation`) but is not described anywhere in ## Scope's description of `run_e2_benchmark.py`, leaving its behavior unspecified.
findings.location: ## Scope (run_e2_benchmark.py description) vs. AC 6 proof command
scenarios: Implementer omits the flag from run_e2_benchmark.py; AC 6 fails with unrecognised argument at evaluation.
Implementer guesses at what self-check-isolation should do (run one pair? check HEAD before/after?) without normative spec text.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `--self-check-isolation` paragraph to the `run_e2_benchmark.py` Scope entry specifying: what single pair it runs (e.g., one control + one mutant from any class), what HEAD-unchanged assertion it makes, and what exit code it emits on success vs. failure.
evidence: Scope describes run_e2_benchmark.py as: 'Clones REAL_REPO/.tickets-tracker → tmp mutant store … journals raw verdicts incrementally … emits e2_baseline.json ONLY when every class is valid' — no mention of `--self-check-isolation`
AC 6 isolation proof: `run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation` is the only reference to this flag
The flag's behavior (which single pair it runs, what it asserts, how it signals pass/fail) is entirely unspecified
findings.finding: The `--self-check-isolation` CLI flag on `run_e2_benchmark.py` is referenced in the AC isolation proof command but is never described in the Scope section's specification of `run_e2_benchmark.py` — its expected behavior, inputs, and pass/fail contract are unspecified, leaving the implementer to infer what the flag must do from a single proof-command line.
findings.location: Scope section (`run_e2_benchmark.py` description) vs. AC 6 isolation proof command: `.venv/bin/python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation`
scenarios: Implementer adds the flag without a shared understanding of its contract, producing a flag that skips write-back but doesn't actually validate HEAD immutability, silently passing AC 6 without real isolation assurance.
A future maintainer reading Scope cannot determine what --self-check-isolation does or whether it is safe to remove.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope description of run_e2_benchmark.py, add: '`--self-check-isolation`: runs one mutant/control pair through the full mechanism (clone → inject → gate → cleanup), then asserts that `git -C REAL_REPO/.tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD` is identical before and after; exits 0 if identical, non-zero (with a diff summary) if not. No corpus or baseline files are written in this mode.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--self-check-isolation` to the Scope description of `run_e2_benchmark.py`, specifying: what it runs (e.g. one synthetic mutant/control pair on a known test ticket), what it checks (real `.tickets-tracker` HEAD is unchanged before/after), and what it exits (0 on success, non-zero on mutation detected).
evidence: AC6 proof command: '`.venv/bin/python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation`'
## Scope description of `run_e2_benchmark.py` describes: clone store, inject defects, run gate, apply classify_pair, write outputs — no mention of `--self-check-isolation` mode
ABSENCE: `--self-check-isolation` appears nowhere in the harnesses directory (no existing file) and nowhere in the plan's Scope deliverable descriptions
findings.finding: The `--self-check-isolation` CLI flag is referenced in AC6's isolation proof command (`run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation`) but is never described in the Scope section's specification of `run_e2_benchmark.py`. An implementer has no contract for what this flag is supposed to do or prove.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC6 (isolation proof); ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` description
scenarios: Implementer writes the harness with `--self-check-isolation` as a no-op or missing entirely — the AC6 proof command fails with an unrecognized argument error
Implementer guesses `--self-check-isolation` should run a single pair and check HEAD — but the expected behavior (single pair? synthetic pair? which ticket?) is unspecified

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the degradation ladder (N≥5 fallback) with AC 1: either update AC 1 to accept N≥5 with an explicit degradation flag in the corpus, or remove the N≥5 path and commit to N=10 via multi-mutant-per-base seeding as the sole fallback.
evidence: Plan: 'If a class still cannot reach 10 distinct real bases, drop to the available N≥5, keep the thresholds as RATES (≥70% detection, ≤10% blocking-FP)'
AC 1 verify: `assert all(m[k]>=10 and m[k]==ctl[k] for k in m), (dict(m),dict(ctl))`
The two sections directly contradict each other on the minimum corpus size.
findings.finding: The degradation ladder in ## What permits dropping to N≥5 rows per class if 10 distinct real bases are unavailable, yet AC 1's verify script unconditionally asserts `m[k]>=10` for all classes — an implementer who invokes the degradation ladder will produce a corpus that fails the AC, creating two conflicting directions.
findings.location: ## What (degradation ladder section) vs. AC 1 (verify script: `assert all(m[k]>=10 ...`)
scenarios: Class 2 or 3 container shortage is flagged as a real risk ('class-2/3 container-shortage risk') — if it materializes, the implementer has no AC-passing path.
Synthetic base plan path (N<5) reports separately — but AC 1 still fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `degradation_n` field to `e2_thresholds.json` and update AC 1's verify script to use `m[k] >= t['classes'][k].get('degradation_n', 10)`, so the pre-registered threshold file is the single source of truth for the minimum N, and the ladder is exercised through it rather than contradicting the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the mechanical mutation algorithm for class 1 (e.g., 'append to the plan's ## What section: "Reuses `<real_module_name>.<method>` which already exposes <fabricated_interface>" where the method does not exist in the real module') and class 4 (e.g., 'append a new scope bullet adding a specific type of mechanism — an event-sourced audit log — that no AC references') so the executor can implement `assemble_e2_mutants.py` deterministically without judgment calls.
evidence: Class 1: 'plan asserts an existing named module provides a capability it lacks' — HOW to select the module and fabricate the false assertion is unspecified
Class 4: 'a change the plan does not need (FixedBench over-action)' — what algorithm identifies and injects an 'unnecessary' change in a real plan is unspecified
Classes 2, 3, 5 have clearer mechanical procedures (re-partition by named layer taxonomy; delete ordering edge; strip tag)
Scope says script 'applies the per-class mechanical mutation' without specifying the algorithm for classes 1/4
findings.finding: The mutation procedures for class 1 (absent asserted capability) and class 4 (unnecessary mechanism) are defined by their end-state but lack a specified mechanical algorithm, leaving the executor to decide how to produce each mutant. Class 1 requires the implementer to choose which module to implicate and what false capability claim to inject; class 4 requires the implementer to decide what constitutes an 'unnecessary' mechanism in a real plan — a judgment that risks producing subjective, non-reproducible mutants.
findings.location: ## What — defect class definitions 1 and 4
scenarios: Two independent implementers produce non-comparable class-1 corpora because one injects a false import reference while the other adds a false method-existence claim
Class-4 mutants vary in character across base plans, making cross-plan discrimination comparison unreliable

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: The mechanism for populating synthetic mutant/control tickets into the throwaway rebar store is unspecified: the plan describes `rebar init` under a tempdir and `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`, but `review_plan(repo_root=<tmp-store>)` calls `_reads.show_ticket(ticket_id, repo_root=<tmp-store>)` which does live event-store reads from `.tickets-tracker` under that root. The mutant corpus rows carry `plan_text`, `children`, and `mutation_diff` but not a rebar event stream, so the implementer cannot know which write API bridges from corpus to store without a spec. Valid options differ non-trivially in complexity and fidelity: `create_ticket` + `edit_ticket` (API path, acquires write locks, preserves schema), `import_tickets` (event-replay import, handles parent-child wiring), or raw event file injection (bypasses locking, fragile). The choice determines whether child tickets and `depends_on` edges (needed for class-2/3 ordering detection by G4) are correctly wired.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` description; also implicitly ## What (corpus schema)
findings.suggested_fix: In `run_e2_benchmark.py`'s description add: 'Populates each pair via `rebar.create_ticket` (parent) + `rebar.create_ticket` (each child) + `rebar.edit_ticket` (description=plan_text) + `rebar.link` (depends_on edges from `children[].deps`); class-3 mutants omit the specific ordering link named in `mutation_diff`. Corpus rows add a `store_ops` sub-field listing the link edges to create/omit per role.'

findings.finding: The unit-test AC for `classify_pair` describes test cases by top-level verdict state ("synthetic PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE verdict dicts") without requiring coverage of the finding-level `decision` field logic — specifically: (a) a target-family finding with `decision=advisory` must count as detected (not just BLOCK-verdict cases), (b) a `decision=block` finding on a NON-target criterion must NOT count as blocking-FP, and (c) a mix of target and non-target findings in one verdict dict. These are the subtle, most-bug-prone branches of the counting rule and are not named in the AC.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — fifth AC bullet (`test_e2_counting.py`); also `## What` AMBIGUOUS/INDETERMINATE section, `classify_pair` spec
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC bullet for `test_e2_counting.py`, replace 'synthetic PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE verdict dicts' with: 'synthetic verdict dicts covering: (i) top-level PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE, (ii) target-family advisory finding on a PASS-verdict mutant (detected=True), (iii) block finding on non-target criterion on a PASS-verdict control (blocking_fp=False), (iv) INDETERMINATE verdict (detected=False, blocking_fp=False), and (v) mixed target+non-target findings in one dict.'

findings.finding: The `classify_pair` counting rule critically depends on per-finding `decision` field values (`block`, `advisory`, `dropped`, `indeterminate`), but the AC5 test description only requires 'synthetic PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE verdict dicts' — top-level verdict states — leaving the most bug-prone branches of the decision protocol untested by specification.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC5 (`test_e2_counting.py`) vs. ## What — AMBIGUOUS/INDETERMINATE counting rule (`classify_pair` spec)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `run_e2_benchmark.py`'s design that gate runs execute SEQUENTIALLY per mutant/control pair (inject → run → journal → restore, then next pair), or alternatively pre-bake all mutant edits into isolated per-pair tmp stores before any gate run begins, to eliminate the inject-then-run TOCTOU race.
evidence: Plan says harness 'injects each seeded defect into the clone via `rebar edit <id> --description=…`' and 'runs the CURRENT gate via the verified mechanism' — sequential inject-then-run per pair is implied but not mandated
All prior harnesses (harness.py:153, exp1_substance.py:91, seedpilot.py:68, etc.) use ThreadPoolExecutor with 4–10 workers
`REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` is read via `os.environ.get()` in `config.tracker_dir()` — a process-global value, not a per-thread ContextVar
The agentic gate run (AGENT-tier criteria G1G2, E4, A1, G6) is multi-turn and long-running, widening the TOCTOU window
findings.finding: The `run_e2_benchmark.py` harness uses a sequential inject-then-run pattern (inject mutant into shared tmp store, then run gate) but never explicitly specifies that gate runs must be sequential. All prior experiment harnesses in this repo use ThreadPoolExecutor for concurrent runs. If the implementer follows that pattern here, a TOCTOU race exists: injecting mutant B's edit into the shared TMP_MUTANT_STORE while gate run A is mid-read (e.g., during the agentic verifier's multi-turn tool calls) could cause run A to observe cross-mutant ticket content, producing a corrupted verdict attributed to the wrong mutant. Since `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` is a process-global OS env var (not a ContextVar), it cannot scope per-concurrent-invocation.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism / ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py
scenarios: Implementer follows the repo's established concurrent-harness pattern, runs 4–10 gate reviews in parallel against the same tmp store; each gate's multi-turn agentic verifier phase may read a different mutant's content than was injected at start of its pair
Class-1 (absent-capability) and class-4 (unnecessary-mechanism) rely on AGENT-tier grounding — the longest-running checks — maximizing the TOCTOU window for cross-contamination
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit statement to the ## Scope `run_e2_benchmark.py` entry: 'Pairs are processed sequentially — no concurrent gate invocations against the same tmp store — to eliminate inject/read races; the crash-resumable journal (skip already-recorded pair_ids on restart) provides fault-tolerance without concurrency.' Alternatively, the assemble step can pre-bake the full tmp store (all mutant edits already injected into separate per-pair sub-dirs), so the run step only reads, never writes, during gate execution.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the closed `mutation_op` enum vocabulary (one value per defect class, e.g. `absent_capability`, `layer_cake_split`, `ordering_edge_delete`, `unnecessary_mechanism`, `untagged_operational_ac`) in ## What alongside the AC1 schema, and add a vocabulary assertion to the AC1 verify script.
evidence: AC1 requires: 'every row carrying … `mutation_op` + `mutation_diff`' for mutant rows
No section in the plan defines the closed vocabulary for `mutation_op` (e.g., 'absent_capability_inject' / 'layer_cake_resplit' / 'ordering_edge_delete' / etc.)
ABSENCE: searching the full plan and repo for 'mutation_op' yields only cache hits from a prior review run, not a definition
Without a defined vocabulary, the AC1 verify script cannot assert correctness of the field value beyond presence
findings.finding: `mutation_op` is a required field in every mutant row (AC1 schema) but its value vocabulary is never defined anywhere in the plan — not as an enum, not as a closed set of strings, not as a mapping from defect class to operation name. An implementer of `assemble_e2_mutants.py` has no contract for what values are valid.
findings.location: ## What — AC1 schema definition: 'every row carrying … `mutation_op` + `mutation_diff`'; ## Scope — `e2_mutant_corpus.jsonl`
scenarios: Two independent implementers produce `mutation_op` values that are semantically equivalent but syntactically different ('layer_cake' vs 'layer-cake-split'), breaking any downstream consumer that keys on the value
The AC1 Python verify script checks only that the key exists, not that it is from a valid set — a bug report field like 'SEEDED' would silently pass
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ## What AC1 schema definition: '`mutation_op` ∈ {`absent_capability`, `layer_cake_split`, `ordering_edge_delete`, `unnecessary_mechanism`, `untagged_operational_ac`} (one value per class 1–5)' and extend the AC1 Python assertion to `assert all(r['mutation_op'] in {'absent_capability','layer_cake_split','ordering_edge_delete','unnecessary_mechanism','untagged_operational_ac'} for r in rows if r['role']=='mutant')`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to `run_e2_benchmark.py` spec: (a) a per-class and total LLM-call cost estimate, (b) an exponential-backoff retry wrapper around each `review_plan` call for retryable API errors (distinguishing retriable HTTP 429/5xx from gate-logic errors), and (c) an optional `--max-workers` flag (default 1/sequential) so parallelism is explicit rather than unspecified.
evidence: 'class 1: agentic', 'class 4: agentic' in the criterion-family table
≥10 mutants × 5 classes × 2 roles = 100+ gate invocations stated in scope
No mention of rate limiting, concurrency cap, or Anthropic API quota handling in run_e2_benchmark.py spec
No LLM-call cost estimate or per-run $ bound anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The benchmark harness will issue ≥100 production gate calls (≥10 mutants × 5 classes + matched controls), of which classes 1 and 4 use the 'agentic' detection tier — each agentic run involves multiple LLM API round-trips. The plan states no rate-limit handling, no concurrency cap, no per-run cost estimate, and no total-run time bound. At even 2–4 API calls per agentic gate run, the agentic-tier classes alone yield 40–80 LLM calls, and a single API rate-limit rejection or quota exhaustion mid-run has no specified recovery path (crash-resume resumes the row but not the failed call within a gate invocation).
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism / Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py
scenarios: API rate limit hit mid-run causes a mid-invocation exception that is not retried, the gate call exits with an exception (not INDETERMINATE), and the harness crashes without journaling that row — leaving the resumable journal with a gap
Accumulated Anthropic API cost for 40–80 agentic calls is non-trivial and unbudgeted
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each `review_plan(...)` call in a retry loop with exponential backoff (e.g., tenacity with `retry=retry_on_exception(is_rate_limit_error), wait=wait_exponential(min=2, max=60), stop=stop_after_attempt(5)`). Journal `{row_id, error, attempt}` for non-retriable failures so they don't silently become INDETERMINATEs. Add a one-line cost note to the README (e.g., 'estimated 200–400 LLM calls at ~$0.05 each ≈ $10–20').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the rationale for `force=True` in the gate-execution mechanism: the idempotent-reuse and drift-refresh paths are already skipped by `sign=False`; document `force=True` as defense-in-depth (in case sign is ever True) rather than the primary skip mechanism.
evidence: plan_review/__init__.py:508: `if sign and not force:` — guard for idempotent reuse; sign=False already bypasses it
plan_review/__init__.py:517: `if sign:` — guard for drift-refresh; sign=False already bypasses it
Plan claims: 'force=True (skip idempotent-reuse / drift-refresh so every mutant runs fresh)' — rationale is inaccurate given sign=False
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'force=True (skip idempotent-reuse / drift-refresh so every mutant runs fresh)' citing __init__.py:508-518 — but both the idempotent-reuse check (line 508: `if sign and not force`) and the drift-refresh check (line 517: `if sign`) are already skipped by `sign=False`. With `sign=False`, `force=True` has no additional effect: the code path reaches the full review regardless. The stated rationale is unbacked by the cited code.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism, 'Non-mutating flags' bullet: 'force=True (skip idempotent-reuse / drift-refresh so every mutant runs fresh) — __init__.py:508-518,570-587'
scenarios: A reader implementing the harness may omit force=True reasoning it is redundant (which it is), then later add sign=True for some reason — at which point the lack of force=True would cause idempotent reuse to fire and silently return cached verdicts for controls, producing a misleading baseline.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'force=True (skip idempotent-reuse / drift-refresh so every mutant runs fresh)' to 'force=True (defense-in-depth: prevents idempotent reuse or drift-refresh short-circuits even if sign is ever enabled; with sign=False both paths are already skipped per __init__.py:508,517)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `target_criteria` field per class to `e2_thresholds.json` (and the AC schema check) so the criterion-family-to-class mapping is externalized, version-controlled alongside the thresholds, and readable without inspecting harness source.
evidence: Plan's table: 'Class 1 absent capability | E4, G1G2, G6 | agentic' — this mapping is in plan prose only
e2_thresholds.json AC schema: `assert all({'detection_min','blocking_fp_max'}<=set(t['classes'][k]) for k in t['classes'])` — no `target_criteria` field required
classify_pair signature: `classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)` — target_criteria is a parameter, meaning run_e2_benchmark.py supplies it, presumably hardcoded
Plan says criteria are 'validated against the live registry at run time' — a safety check, not an externalization
findings.finding: The target-criterion-family-to-defect-class mapping (e.g., class 1 → E4/G1G2/G6, class 2 → G3/G4) — which `run_e2_benchmark.py` must pass as `target_criteria` to `classify_pair` — is specified only in a prose table in ## What, not in `e2_thresholds.json` or any other externalized config. When R1/R3/R4 add new criteria, the harness must be edited by hand and the mapping is not verifiable from the committed artifacts alone.
findings.location: ## What — criterion-family table; Scope — `e2_counting.py` (`classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)`); `runs/e2_thresholds.json` schema AC
scenarios: R3 ships a new criterion `G5` for layer-cake detection; the harness still only checks G3/G4 for class 2, silently under-counting detection; the baseline table shows weak class-2 detection because the new criterion's findings are not attributed.
A reviewer cannot reproduce the criterion mapping from the committed artifacts alone (corpus + thresholds.json + baseline.json) without reading harness source.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to e2_thresholds.json schema: `{"classes": {"1": {"detection_min": 0.8, "blocking_fp_max": 0.1, "target_criteria": ["E4", "G1G2", "G6"]}, ...}}` and update the AC python one-liner to assert `target_criteria` is a non-empty list for each class. run_e2_benchmark.py reads target_criteria from the file instead of hardcoding.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `run_e2_benchmark.py` that API/infrastructure exceptions from `review_plan` are caught separately from gate-emitted INDETERMINATE verdicts: log them as `verdict='ERROR'`, do NOT count them as INDETERMINATE, journal them with `{row_id, error_type, traceback}`, and after the run report the error count separately — halting or warning when errors exceed a configurable threshold (e.g., >5% of rows).
evidence: 'INDETERMINATE ⇒ gate did not act: … counts as NOT DETECTED (FN) on a mutant' — the counting rule applies to gate-emitted INDETERMINATE verdicts, but the plan conflates this with the execution-error case
No error-handling or exception-classification spec for `review_plan` call failures in run_e2_benchmark.py
run_validity guard: 'a degenerate high-abstain run would spuriously read as perfect FP-avoidance' — the guard targets genuine abstains but infrastructure errors have the same effect
findings.finding: The plan's INDETERMINATE counting rule (INDETERMINATE ⇒ FN on mutants, non-FP on controls) does not distinguish between a VALID gate abstain and an API/infrastructure error that causes the gate call to throw an exception. If `review_plan(...)` raises (e.g., Anthropic 500, network timeout, missing env var), the harness has no specified behavior — the exception could propagate uncaught, or if caught generically, be silently logged as INDETERMINATE, which would inflate the INDETERMINATE fraction and potentially trigger the 0.20-cap invalidity guard for the wrong reason, or — if the gate is not retried — silently inflate FN counts.
findings.location: ## What — AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling; Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py
scenarios: Anthropic API returns 503 on row 47; harness catches generic Exception, records verdict='INDETERMINATE', INDETERMINATE fraction for that class rises above 0.20, run_validity marks the class invalid and refuses to emit e2_baseline.json — the run is invalidated by an infrastructure hiccup, not a gate behavior
Worse: exception is not caught, harness crashes, journal is incomplete, and the run must restart from scratch despite crash-resume design
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `ERROR` sentinel verdict to the harness journal (not the gate's own vocabulary): `try: v = review_plan(...) except Exception as e: journal({..., 'verdict': 'ERROR', 'error': str(e)}); continue`. In `run_validity` treat ERROR rows as neither INDETERMINATE nor valid verdicts, and emit a separate `error_count` per class in `e2_run_validity.json`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `run_e2_benchmark.py` a defined RETIRE path for TMP_MUTANT_STORE: either (a) use `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory` with explicit `__exit__` cleanup registered via `atexit` so it is removed even on normal exit (though not SIGKILL), or (b) use a deterministic path with a `--reuse-store` flag that allows crash-resume against an existing clone and an explicit `--clean` flag that deletes it first, and document which mode is default.
evidence: 'harness copies REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT/.tickets-tracker → TMP_MUTANT_STORE (a full clone)' — creation described
No mention of `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory`, `shutil.rmtree`, or any cleanup step for TMP_MUTANT_STORE after the run
The isolation AC only checks that the REAL store HEAD is unchanged; it does not address tmp-store deletion
findings.finding: The tmp mutant store (`TMP_MUTANT_STORE` — a full clone of `.tickets-tracker`) has no specified RETIRE/cleanup path. The plan describes CREATE (harness clones real store) and CONSUME (gate reads via `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR`), but never says when or how the tmp directory is deleted. If the harness uses a fixed path (e.g., `/tmp/e2_mutant_store`), failed or repeated runs accumulate stale clones; if it uses a Python `tempfile` context manager, this is implicit but unspecified and won't be cleaned up on SIGKILL.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism; Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py
scenarios: Developer runs benchmark twice; second run finds a stale TMP_MUTANT_STORE from a previous crashed run and either overwrites it (losing crash-resume state) or fails with a 'directory exists' error
CI runner accumulates large tmp clones across multiple E2 runs, filling disk
findings.suggested_fix: Use `tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix='e2_mutant_store_')` and register `atexit.register(shutil.rmtree, tmp_dir, ignore_errors=True)`. For crash-resume across SIGKILL, store the tmp path in `runs/e2_active_store.txt` at start, check for it at startup, and offer `--resume` to reuse it and `--clean` to delete and restart.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/experiments/` to the Scope section as a new directory to create, and specify whether an `__init__.py` is needed for pytest collection (check the repo's `pytest.ini`/`pyproject.toml` testpaths configuration).
evidence: Directory listing of `tests/unit/` shows no `experiments/` subdirectory — only `workflow/` is a subdirectory alongside the flat `.py` files.
The scope bullet reads: `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py — CI-collectable golden test...` but does not mention creating the parent directory or any required `__init__.py`.
findings.finding: The plan places a new test file at `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py`, but the `tests/unit/experiments/` subdirectory does not exist in the repo (only `tests/unit/` and `tests/unit/workflow/` are present). The scope section does not enumerate creation of this directory, and pytest discovery may require an `__init__.py` (or at minimum a `conftest.py`) for the new subdirectory to be picked up by the CI runner.
findings.location: ## Scope — `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py`
scenarios: CI runs `pytest tests/unit/` and silently skips or fails to collect `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py` because `experiments/` has no `__init__.py` (if the project uses package-based test discovery) or because the directory simply doesn't exist at implementation time.
An implementer creates the file without the directory, causing an immediate filesystem error.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a scope bullet: `tests/unit/experiments/__init__.py` (empty, new directory marker). Alternatively, verify the repo uses `rootdir`-based pytest discovery (no `__init__.py` needed) and note that explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or supplement the `passes.py:269,514` citations with accurate pointers to where agentic code-grounding actually occurs (e.g., `registry.py:66` `CODEBASE_GROUNDED`, the `AGENT_TIER` frozenset, or `workflow_ops.py` where agent steps run), so the mechanism claim is verifiable from the cited locations.
evidence: Line 269 of `passes.py`: `prompt = prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` — this resolves a prompt template, not a code-grounding read.
Line 514 of `passes.py`: `prompt = prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` — ISF log summarizer, not an agentic finder.

The CODEBASE_GROUNDED set in `registry.py:66` (`frozenset({'E4', 'G1G2', 'A1', 'G6'})`) and AGENT_TIER confirm those criteria ARE agentic, but the actual grounding calls happen in the agent tool loop at runtime, not at those two lines.
findings.finding: The plan cites `passes.py:269,514` as evidence that 'agentic finders read the REAL modules' for class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding. However, line 269 is `prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` (inside `_resolve_system`, a prompt-resolution helper) and line 514 is `prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', ...)` (inside `summarize_for_isf`, an ISF log-compressor). Neither is the agentic tool-loop invocation that actually reads modules; the actual code-grounding happens through the agent's file-tool calls at runtime. The cited line numbers are misleading anchors for the code-grounding claim.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism paragraph: '(the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`))'
scenarios: A reviewer or implementer looking up `passes.py:269,514` to verify the grounding mechanism finds prompt-resolution boilerplate, not file-reading code, and cannot confirm the cited claim from those lines.
If passes.py is refactored and those line numbers shift, the citation breaks without any semantic connection to the claim.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that calling `review_plan(id, repo_root=<tmp-store>, source='local', sign=False, emit_sidecar=False)` causes 'ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout.' This is false per the codebase. In local mode, `gate_source.gate_read_root` does not activate `use_tickets_root` or `use_code_root` (it yields bare at line 116-117). Ticket reads in `_assemble_context_uncached` use `repo_root=repo_root` (the tmp store — correct). But the code-grounding root (`PlanContext.repo_root`) is also resolved via `resolve_code_root(repo_root, cfg_repo_path=cfg.repo_path, allow_checkout_fallback=False)` — and since `repo_root=<tmp-store>` is truthy it wins the cascade at step 1, rooting code-grounding at the tmp store too. The tmp store is an empty `rebar init` directory with no source files, so agentic criteria (E4, G1G2, G6) would read an empty codebase — crippling the class-1/4 detection measurements the benchmark is designed to produce.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` description: 'ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout'
findings.suggested_fix: In `run_e2_benchmark.py`, do not pass `repo_root=<tmp-store>` to `review_plan`. Instead pass `repo_root=<real-checkout>` (the actual source tree) and direct ticket reads to the tmp store by constructing a `LLMConfig` with `tickets_path=<tmp-store-tracker-dir>` and using `use_tickets_root(<tmp-store>)` context — mirroring how `opcert_service/jobs.py` isolates ticket writes while keeping code reads on the real checkout.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that calling `review_plan(ticket_id, repo_root=<tmp-store>, source='local', ...)` will route ticket reads to the tmp store while 'code grounding reads the real in-place checkout.' This is unverified and appears incorrect: with source='local', gate_source does NOT set a separate code root context variable — `repo_root` is passed to both `_assemble_context_uncached` (which passes it to `resolve_code_root` for code grounding AND to `_reads.show_ticket` for ticket reads). A tmp-store containing materialized tickets but no source code would cause all code-grounding criteria (P2 file-reference checks, P3 dependency checks, and the agentic E4/G6/A1 criteria) to silently degrade or abstain, producing non-production-faithful reviews that undercount defect detection.
findings.location: ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py description: '(ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `source='attested'` with a local snapshot, OR — since attested requires a real SHA — seed the tmp store as a git repo that symlinks/copies the real source tree for code grounding while containing the fabricated tickets. Alternatively, confirm whether passing `tickets_path=<tmp-store>` as a config override (as `apply_handle` does in attested mode) would route ticket reads separately while `repo_root=<real-checkout>` grounds code.

findings.finding: The `run_e2_benchmark.py` harness silently depends on `review_plan(repo_root=<tmp-store>)` routing ALL ticket reads to the tmp-store while code-grounding reads the real in-place checkout — a split-read behavior that is an undocumented coupling assumption about the internal API. If `review_plan` uses `repo_root` for both ticket reads AND code grounding (or neither), the harness either contaminates the real `.tickets-tracker` branch or runs code grounding against stale/empty state, producing silently wrong baseline numbers with no AC catching it.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` paragraph: 'ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout'
findings.suggested_fix: In `run_e2_benchmark.py`, after the first review_plan call, assert that (a) no new files appeared in the real `.tickets-tracker` path and (b) at least one finding in `e2_verdicts.jsonl` has a criterion from {E4, G1, G2, G6} with non-null `code_ref` pointing to the real checkout — this double-checks the split. Alternatively, add a one-line comment citing the exact __init__.py line/parameter that implements the split.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `review_plan(id, repo_root=<tmp-store>, source="local", ...)` provides a split-read where 'ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout' — but this is architecturally contradicted by the source. In `local` mode, `gate_read_root` sets NO ContextVars; `resolve_code_root(repo_root=<tmp-store>, ...)` returns `str(<tmp-store>)` at cascade step 1 (the truthy `repo_root` wins immediately), routing code grounding to the empty tmp-store, not the real checkout. Criteria E4, G1G2, G6 — which ground against real source files — would evaluate against the synthetic codebase, silently producing vacuous results for classes 1 and 4 (the classes R1 and R4 depend on).
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` description: 'ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
